---
title: "About"
subtitle: "Building an evidence-based scientific position on precautionary management of jumbo flying squid (*Dosidicus gigas*) within SPRFMO."
format: html
---

## Context

Over the past three years, **CALAMASUR** has built a consolidated scientific
position in favour of precautionary management of the jumbo flying squid
fishery under the **South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
(SPRFMO)**.

Recent discussions within the SPRFMO Scientific Committee aimed at reducing
fishing effort on jumbo squid have produced measures that do not explicitly
guarantee a reduction in fishing pressure on the species. The work described on
this site exists to reopen that discussion on evidence-based terms, and to
continue CALAMASUR's scientific contribution to the elements that underpin
management decisions for this fishery.

## Where the science stands

CALAMASUR presented an improved assessment of jumbo squid to the SPRFMO
Scientific Committee in 2025 (**SC13-Obs03**), covering a range of exploitation
scenarios in response to technical observations raised by Committee members.

::: {.highlight}
The results showed that, although the population trend is **not necessarily
declining**, jumbo squid is subject to a **high exploitation rate**.
:::

Because these results do not coincide with the assessments presented by
[Chile](docs/SC13-SQ06-CHL-SPiCT-summary.qmd) and
[China](docs/SC13-SQ07-CHN-state-space-summary.qmd), it has proved difficult to
agree a consensus exploitation status supported by model results — the problem
examined in detail in the [working paper](docs/modelos.qmd) and the
[SCW18 presentation](slides.qmd).

Refining the CALAMASUR model further — distributing effort across fleets,
discriminating differences in population parameters such as natural mortality —
remains necessary. But even a model that answers most of the Committee's
questions would not, on its own, resolve the deeper need: **identifying and
adopting a base model** against which exploitation status is determined.

## Three lines of action

Given the complexity of the decision process and the technical disagreements
between the squid models, three **non-exclusive** lines of action are proposed.
Each aims to give Scientific Committee members information that complements —
rather than replaces — the population models currently in play.

::: {.model-cards}

<a class="model-card" href="docs/modelos.qmd">
<span class="model-card-tag">Line 1 · Under way</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Multi-model risk analysis</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">Weights population projections from a set of models (CALAMASUR, Chile, China) under differing assumptions about fishing effort. Its defining feature is the capacity to incorporate the views of a broad group of stakeholders, and to weight the credibility of each projection on data, empirical evidence and expert judgement.</span>
</a>

<a class="model-card" href="slides.qmd">
<span class="model-card-tag">Line 2 · Proposed</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Empirical Management Strategy Evaluation</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">Applies the technical and methodological basis of a standard MSE to test, by simulation, how different models respond to alternative harvest control rules. Models that lack robustness show little sensitivity across control rules — and can therefore be set aside.</span>
</a>

<a class="model-card" href="docs/SC13-Obs03-CALAMASUR-summary.qmd">
<span class="model-card-tag">Line 3 · Committed</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Improving the CALAMASUR model</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">Delivers the commitments made in SC13-Obs03: widening the fishing units (fleets) represented, and capturing differences in natural mortality between zones.</span>
</a>

:::

### How the three fit together

The three lines answer different questions, and are deliberately not
alternatives to one another:

| | Question it answers | Relationship to the models |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-model risk analysis** | *Given that the models disagree, what can we still say with confidence?* | Carries all three models as weighted hypotheses |
| **Empirical MSE** | *Which models are robust enough to inform a decision?* | Tests the models against harvest control rules |
| **Model improvement** | *Can the remaining objections to CALAMASUR's model be closed out?* | Improves one model |

: {tbl-colwidths="[24,40,36]"}

Line 1 is the most developed: it is set out in full in the
[working paper](docs/modelos.qmd), which defines risk following FAO guidance as
"the probability of something undesirable happening", and shows how competing
hypotheses are hierarchized, weighted, and combined into probability statements
that can seed a harvest control rule.

## Implementation {#implementation}

The immediate objective is to develop **at least one** of these lines far
enough to present an implementation scheme — structured along the lines of
terms of reference — to the **14th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee
(SC14, September 2026)**.

The lines differ in complexity and in the level of technical dedication they
demand. For SC14 the realistic aim is therefore to set out the basis for
implementation schematically, rather than to deliver all three in full.

The work is organised around three steps:

::: {.model-cards}

<span class="model-card">
<span class="model-card-tag">Step 1 · Before the meeting</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Technical document</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">Preparation of the technical document, together with the supporting material used to present it — agreed with the counterpart before submission.</span>
</span>

<span class="model-card">
<span class="model-card-tag">Step 2 · During</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Representation at SC14</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">Scientific representation of CALAMASUR through the meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee, September 2026.</span>
</span>

<span class="model-card">
<span class="model-card-tag">Step 3 · After</span>
<span class="model-card-title">Post-meeting review</span>
<span class="model-card-desc">A retrospective assessment of how the position was received, used to identify and scope the next round of technical actions.</span>
</span>

:::

A detailed explanation of the methodological schemes behind each line of action
can be provided and discussed on request.

## Who

This work is carried out by **Juan-Carlos Quiroz**
([ORCID](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2831-7689){target="_blank"}) with
**Renato Gozzer**
([ORCID](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1156-4552){target="_blank"}), and is funded
by [CALAMASUR](https://www.calamasur.com){target="_blank"} with support from
[Facile Visual](https://www.facilevisual.com){target="_blank"} and the
Innovations for Ocean Action Foundation.

The working paper is archived and citable at
[10.5281/zenodo.21361536](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21361536){target="_blank"}.
